sections.content: 4.000 4.563 TO 4.683 4.750 Table 6: Additional Board 4 leaderboard for selected models. The metric columns follow the automatic and MLLM-based result tables in the main paper. TS denotes voice timbre similarity between the generated speech and the provided audio reference. slightly stronger on visual and audio task following, while Kling 3.0 performs better on speech content accuracy and temporal order following. Overall, Board 4 preserves the same broad pattern as the main benchmark: Seedance 2.0 is more balanced across reference preservation, structural quality, and audio-reference use, while Kling 3.0 remains competitive on selected instruction and source-attribution dimensions. Case Analyses This section provides additional case-level and board-level analyses for representative metrics. These analyses complement the metric construction details below by showing how the evaluation protocol behaves under different board settings and how rejudging corrects inconsistent checklist judgments. D.1 Entity Fidelity Analysis We further analyze Entity Fidelity (EF) across the three boards to understand how reference complexity affects visual preservation. Board 1 obtains the highest average EF score of 0.6930, while Board 2 and Board 3 drop to 0.5893 and 0.5748, respectively. Branch-level results show that this decline is mainly driven by the Human branch: average Human EF decreases from 0.6930 on Board 1 to 0.5522 on Board 2 and 0.5409 on Board 3, whereas Object and Background scores remain relatively stable around 0.650.68. This indicates that the harder boards primarily stress human identity, face/body appearance, and temporal consistency under heterogeneous composition, interaction, occlusion, and pose variation, rather than simply making object or background preservation harder. Meanwhile, this drop may also be affected by metric sensitivity: multi-person interactions are more likely to produce profile faces, smaller faces, or partial occlusions, which may introduce downward bias in face/body similarity scores. At the model level, stronger systems such as Kling and Seedance remain relatively stable across boards and rank among the top models on all three board totals. Other models exhibit different branch-level weaknesses: some are more limited by human consistency, while others are affected by object or background preservation. Overall, these patterns suggest that board-level Figure 10: Representative Entity Fidelity case. This case illustrates the operation of EF tools. EF differences reflect both increased task difficulty and model-specific robustness. As multi-reference composition and entity interaction become more complex, maintaining human consistency becomes the dominant challenge for current R2AV systems. D.2 Speech-Lip Synchronization Analysis The pre-filter removes videos with large head motion, invisible speaking faces, off-screen speech, or insufficient mouth visibility, but residual motion can still affect SyncNet-based scores. The retained sample count also differs across models: Gemini-Omni, which often produces stable frontal faces, retains 84 valid videos, while more dynamic models such as Kling and Seedance 2.0 retain only 47 valid videos. These results suggest that future lip-sync evaluation should develop more robust synchronization models that can tolerate dynamic motion, profile faces, occlusions, and other challenging speaking conditions. The board-level results suggest that speech-lip synchronization evaluation becomes less stable as the generation setting becomes more complex. Board 1 retains the largest number of valid samples after the stable-frontal and temporal-offset filtering, indicating that it contains more visible and stable speaking-face cases. In contrast, Board 2 and Board 3 retain substantially fewer valid samples across models, making their VSLS scores more sensitive to sample selection. Although Board 2 can sometimes achieve high scores on the retained videos, these scores should be interpreted cautiously due to the limited number of valid samples. Board 3 is generally the most challenging setting, with the lowest retention counts and more cases affected by multi-entity interactions, pose variation, and reduced mouth visibility. Therefore, VSLS should be analyzed together with board type and retained sample count, rather than treated as purely model-intrinsic synchronization score. Speech Content Accuracy Analysis. We analyze Speech Content Accuracy (SCA) according to the requested speech language. At the aggregate level, English and Chinese achieve nearly identical strict accuracy, with 87.8% for English and 88.0% for Chinese, suggesting that the benchmark does not strongly favor either language overall. However, model-level results reveal clear language-specific behaviors. Kling achieves 90.6% accuracy on English and 97.6% on Chinese, while HappyHouse 1.1 reaches 89.9% on English and 100.0% on Chinese. Their remaining errors are mainly mild English-side failures, including extra words, repeated phrases, natural paraphrases, and occasional omission of required English content. Seedance 2.0 also performs strongly in both languages, with 93.5% English accuracy and 91.2% Chinese accuracy, but exhibits occasional language-control and content-control errors. Some Chinese prompts are rendered in English or another foreign language, some English prompts are generated in Chinese, and some outputs introduce additional speech content beyond the required utterance. Gemini-Omni shows the strongest English performance, reaching 98.1%, but its Chinese accuracy drops to 76.7%. Its Chinese-side failures are mainly caused by word substitutions, semantic drift, missing speech, unintelligible speech, and small number of unrelated English outputs. In contrast, Wan and Vidu are less stable across both languages. Wan obtains 82.6% English accuracy and 84.0% Chinese accuracy, while Vidu obtains 74.1% and 75.0%, respectively. Their errors are dominated by missing speech, irrelevant utterances, and occasional Figure 11: Board-level Speech-Lip Synchronization analysis. (a) SLS scores across the three boards for Gemini-Omni, Kling, and Seedance 2.0, grouped by model. (b) Number of valid videos retained after filtering. translation of Chinese requirements into English. Gemini-Omni has more Chinese-side errors, including additions, repetitions, unrelated dialogue, English substitution for Chinese prompts, and semantic reversal. These results indicate that SCA measures more than whether model can synthesize speech. It also captures whether the model preserves the requested language, lexical content, dialogue assignment, and prompt-specified utterance structure. This motivates evaluating SCA separately from speech-lip synchronization and audio quality: model may generate fluent and synchronized speech while still failing to reproduce the required spoken content. D.3 Rejudging Cases Analysis Table 7 presents two representative rejudging cases. The first case shows that the initial judge can be overly literal. For the checklist question Does someone adjust the position of @Image2?, several outputs were initially scored as failures because no explicit adjusting action was identified. However, picking up, carrying, or placing the plant changes its position and satisfies the semantic intent of the checklist item. The rejudge stage therefore corrects these overly strict judgments. The second case illustrates the opposite failure mode. For Is the scene set backstage of theater?, some initial scores were too high because the judge accepted general theater-stage setting as backstage. The rejudge stage lowers these scores when the evidence indicates main stage or an ambiguous theater context rather than clear backstage setting. Together, these cases show that rejudging improves consistency by aligning scores with both the intended checklist semantics and comparable visual evidence across models. D.4 Stability of MLLM-Dependent Evaluation To assess evaluation stability, we randomly sample 60 examples from the three main boards and repeat the complete MLLMdependent evaluation pipeline five times using the same generated outputs and evaluation settings. EF and SLS are also reported because they incorporate MLLM-assisted processing: EF uses an MLLM-estimated paste-naturalness factor for posthoc calibration, while SLS uses MLLM-based pre-screening to identify samples suitable for reliable lip-sync measurement. As summarized in Table 8, the relative standard deviations remain below 1% across all metrics, while the item-level MAEs are consistently small. These results suggest that the reported evaluation outcomes are not artifacts of judge stochasticity and that the overall model comparisons remain reliable under repeated evaluation. Detailed Metric Constructions and Prompts This section details the construction of all evaluation metrics, including the generation of metric-specific checklists, the prompts used for MLLM-as-a-Judge evaluation, and the implementation details of automatic metrics. For readability, the prompt boxes below present the core components of each evaluation prompt, while omitting output-format specifications and implementationspecific serialization details. Figure 12: Language-dependent performance in Speech Content Accuracy. The figure reports strict accuracy on English and Chinese speech items for each model. E.1 Basic Quality Anatomical Quality (AQ)

sections.title: Anatomical and Object Integrity Judge Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Anatomical Knowledge / Object Integrity Judge 2 3 You are computer vision expert specialized in biomechanics, rigid-body physics, and 3D scene understanding. 4 5 Your task is to evaluate whether generated video exhibits physically plausible structure, correct spatial relationships, true 3D scene integration, and stable object attachment behavior. Output an Object Integrity Score (OIS). 6 7 ## Style Neutrality Rule 8 9 Do not penalize video merely because it is intentionally cartoon, anime, illustration, 2D, stylized, or non-photorealistic. Judge physical consistency within the chosen style. Only penalize visible failures such as deformation, melting, impossible articulation, object duplication, sudden disappearance/appearance, floating, penetration, broken attachment, inconsistent shadows/contact, or implausible object motion. 10 11 ## Evaluation Dimensions 12 13 **1. Biological / Structural Consistency** - limb proportion stability, impossible joint articulation, facial deformation, biomechanical plausibility. 14 15 **2. Rigid Body Stability** - deformation of cars/buildings/furniture, contour stability under motion, structural consistency across frames, absence of melting or Question Does someone adjust the position of @Image2? Model Kling 3.0 Seedance 2.0 Gemini-Omni 1.0 1.0 1. Is the scene set backstage of theater? Gemini-Omni 1.0 Seedance 2.0 Wan 2.7 5. 4.0 Before After Rejudge Rationale 5.0 5.0 4.0 2. 3.0 2.0 Picking up and placing the plant in new location effectively changes its position, satisfying the intended action. The woman picks up, carries, and places the plant, which should be treated as valid position adjustment. The plant is picked up and held, which partially changes its position, although it is not clearly placed in new location. The video shows theater main-stage context but lacks clear backstage evidence, such as off-stage props, exposed structures, or backstage space. The original reason describes the scene as on or backstage, indicating ambiguity rather than clear backstage setting. The scene suggests theater environment, but the evidence points to the main stage rather than distinct backstage area. Table 7: Representative rejudging cases. The rejudge stage corrects both overly strict and overly permissive initial judgments by checking whether the score is consistent with the visual evidence and the checklist semantics. Dimension Basic Quality Reference Consistency Audio-Visual Consistency Instruction Following Metric AQ EF BC DP SLS ESM SC VT AT SCA TO Score 3.655 0.022 0.598 0.005 4.382 0.035 4.417 0. 3.121 0.014 4.768 0.031 4.846 0.009 4.274 0.039 4.626 0.046 4.712 0.014 4.221 0.030 MAE 0.137 0.014 0.094 0.101 0.082 0.067 0. 0.116 0.098 0.087 0.128 Table 8: Stability of MLLM-dependent evaluation over five repeated runs on 60-sample subset. Score reports the mean and standard deviation of the five run-level average scores. MAE denotes the average item-level absolute difference over all pairs of runs. wobbling. 16 17 **3. Physical Attachment & Spatial Plausibility** - correct physical contact, absence of floating/penetration/detachment, correct scale, object permanence failures (duplicated objects, ghost copies), physically implausible motion (teleportation, abrupt jumps, unexplained sliding). 18 19 **4. 3D Scene Integration & Photorealism** - sticker-like artifacts breaking contact/occlusion/spatial relations, missing shadows when the style otherwise uses them, lighting consistency, correct occlusion and depth coherence. 20 21 ## Scoring (1-5) 22 23 - 1: Severe physical failure, broken attachment, impossible object permanence, or strong compositing artifacts 24 - 2: Major structural or physical inconsistency 25 - 3: Noticeable issues but still recognizable 26 - 4: Mostly stable with minor artifacts 27 - 5: Fully physically consistent and coherent within the established style E.2 Reference Consistency Entity Fidelity (EF) Entity Fidelity (EF) measures whether the entities in generated video remain visually faithful to their corresponding reference images. The metric combines automated reference-similarity branches with MLLM-based diagnostic checks. For each generated video, we first parse the sample metadata, load the corresponding human, object, and scene references, uniformly sample 32 frames, and compute branch-level similarity scores. For human fidelity, we adopt different pipelines for real and cartoon subjects. For real-human references, the face branch detects faces using InsightFace and extracts ElasticFace R100 embeddings. The body branch detects person regions with YOLO-World and uses SigLIP embeddings as the final body similarity score. When both face and body evidence are available, the frame-level real-human score combines them with default face weight of 0.6. For cartoon subjects, we use YOLOv8 anime-face detector for the head branch and compute DINOv2-based head similarity, since real-face recognition models are unreliable for stylized faces. The cartoon body branch also uses YOLO-World crops and SigLIP embeddings as the final body score. Before combining head and body evidence, low-quality cartoon body matches with similarity below 0.6 are filtered out. For object fidelity, we use YOLO-World open-vocabulary detection to localize referenced objects in sampled video frames and compute SigLIP similarity between the cropped detections and the reference object image. For scene fidelity, we compare full-frame SigLIP embeddings with the scene reference and use the best-matching sampled frame, since this branch targets global environment similarity rather than localized object appearance. To account for temporal drift, the automated EF score is further adjusted by Long-Term Consistency (LTC). Given the frame-level EF sequence, LTC measures score fluctuation, collapse ratio, and late-stage degradation relative to early frames, and converts these factors into stability coefficient in [0, 1]. The adjusted score is defined as EFbase = EF (0.7 + 0.3 LTC) where EF denotes the mean automated EF score over sampled frames. Thus, videos with stable identity and appearance over time keep scores close to the original mean, while videos with identity drift, disappearance, or late-frame degradation receive lower EF score. In addition, the paste-naturalness coefficient is used as correction to automated EF by assigning visual-integration penalty to videos where the subject appears sticker-like. Overall, automated EF captures reference similarity, LTC captures temporal stability, and the MLLM components provide semantic and compositional diagnostics when high embedding similarity alone is insufficient. The final score is: EFfinal = EFbase Ppaste(r)

sections.title: Compositing Artifact Judge Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Paste Artifact Judge 2 3 You are evaluating video generation artifacts. 4 5 Focus only on paste-like, cutout-like, sticker-like, paper-doll-like, or compositing artifacts in the generated video. Judge whether the main human subject and important foreground objects look physically integrated into the generated scene across time. 6 7 Do not reward identity similarity, reference similarity, object similarity, prompt following, aesthetics, camera style, or high detail. 8 9 ## What To Judge 10 11 - Cutout/sticker-like edges around the person or object 12 - Boundary halo, matte edge, mask edge, or segmentation artifact 13 - Flat paper-like body, face, clothing, or object appearance 14 - Pasted foreground that does not share lighting, shadow, perspective, blur, or depth with the background 15 - Person/object sliding over the scene instead of moving naturally inside it 16 - Inconsistent occlusion between subject, object, and background 17 - Rigid planar subject while the camera/background moves 18 19 ## Do Not Judge 20 21 Identity/reference similarity; prompt following; face beauty; clothing color accuracy; object category correctness; action correctness; anatomical correctness unless it creates paste/cutout artifact; general blur or detail loss unless it directly creates paste-like integration problems. 22 23 ## Checklist 24 25 - P1: Main subject/object boundaries naturally integrated without cutout edges, halos, or segmentation artifacts? 26 - P2: Subject/object lighting, shadows, blur, perspective, and depth match the scene naturally? 27 - P3: Subject/object moves as part of the scene without sliding like pasted layer? 28 - P4: Occlusion and contact with environment natural, without foreground/background separation errors? 29 - P5: Subject/object maintains volumetric, physically integrated appearance? 30 31 ## Scoring (1-5) 32 33 - 5: No obvious paste/cutout artifact. Natural integration throughout. 34 - 4: Minor artifact visible briefly, overall integration acceptable. 35 - 3: Moderate artifact. Subject/object sometimes looks cut out, flat, sliding, or poorly integrated. 36 - 2: Severe artifact. Main subject often looks like sticker, cutout, or pasted texture. 37 - 1: Critical artifact. Subject/object obviously pasted in or physically disconnected for most of the video. 38 39 Strict caps: clear cutout/halo in multiple frames max 3. Flat paper-layer appearance max 3. Visible sliding over background max 3. Severely pasted/disconnected max 2. When uncertain between two scores, choose the lower. Detail Preservation (DP) Fine-Grained Detail Preservation Judge Prompt 1 # Detail Preservation Judge 2 3 You are an expert evaluator for reference-based video generation. 4 5 Your task is to evaluate **fine-grained visual detail preservation** that may not be captured by automatic face recognition, object matching, or background similarity metrics. Focus only on visual details from the reference images. Do not evaluate general prompt following, action correctness, audio quality, lip sync, or temporal quality. 6 7 ## Applicability 8 9 - Evaluate human_detail if has_human_reference = true. 10 - Evaluate object_detail if has_object_reference = true. 11 - Evaluate background_detail if has_background_reference = true. 12 - If detail is not present in the reference image, mark it [N/A], not [YES]. 13 - If the prompt explicitly instructs changing specific detail, mark only that item [SKIP]. 14 15 ## Label Standard 16 17 - [YES]: clearly visible and faithfully preserved. 18 - [PARTIAL]: roughly preserved but incomplete, simplified, blurred, unstable, or inconsistent. 19 - [NO]: missing, replaced, clearly wrong, or severely distorted. 20 - [N/A]: not present in the reference or not applicable. 21 - [SKIP]: explicitly instructed to change by the prompt. 22 23 ## Checklist 24 25 **Human Detail** 26 - H1: Hairstyle, hair color, headwear, makeup, beard, tattoos, scars, moles, badges, or character-specific body structure. 27 - H2: Clothing type, color, outfit structure, and major clothing layout. 28 - H3: Local details - patterns, logos, buttons, collars, belts, jewelry, glasses, bags, gloves, or handheld items. 29 30 **Object Detail** 31 - O1: Object shape, silhouette, geometry, and major structural parts. 32 - O2: Colors, color layout, materials, and textures. 33 - O3: Distinctive details - logos, labels, patterns, laces, handles, seams, absence of melting or deformation. 34 35 **Background Detail** 36 - BG1: Key background elements - buildings, furniture, trees, vehicles, doors, windows, walls, floors, signs. 37 - BG2: Under camera movement or viewpoint change, visible regions still match corresponding reference regions. 38 - BG3: Spatial layout, color/lighting, material textures, and distinctive details - signage, landmarks, architectural details. 39 40 ## Scoring (1-5 per section) 41 42 - 5: All salient details clearly preserved and stable. 43 - 4: Most details preserved, only minor issues. 44 - 3: Some details preserved, several visible inconsistencies. 45 - 2: Only few details preserved; many salient details wrong or missing. 46 - 1: Almost no reference details preserved, or category missing/replaced. 47 48 Hard rules: any salient [NO] section score 4. Multiple salient [PARTIAL] section score usually 4. Main reference missing/replaced section score 2. Final score = arithmetic mean of non-null section scores. Binding Correctness (BC)

sections.title: Binding Correctness Judge Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Binding Correctness Judge 2 3 You are an expert in evaluating reference-based video generation, specializing in visual binding between referenced subjects, outfits, props, objects, and actions. 4 5 Given one or more reference images, the text prompt, and generated video, evaluate whether binding relations are correctly assigned to their intended subjects/objects. Use the text prompt only to identify intended bindings (which subject wears which outfit, which prop belongs to whom, which subject performs which action). 6 7 **This metric evaluates visual binding only.** Do not evaluate audio or sound-source binding. 8 9 ## Do Not Evaluate 10 11 Face-to-body assignment; pure color matching or minor color shifts; general visual fidelity, texture stability, or shape deformation; physical realism issues; camera cuts or temporal smoothness; general instruction following, anatomical correctness, audio, or video quality. 12 13 ## Evaluation Checklist 14 15 ### Identity Binding 16 - **I2**: Are subject identities maintained consistently across frames? 17 - **I3**: When multiple subjects are present, can each be clearly distinguished? 18 19 ### Attribute Binding 20 - **B1**: Are clothing/outfit attributes correctly matched to their respective subjects? 21 - **B3**: Are accessories, props, or handheld objects correctly attached to their intended owner? 22 23 ### Action Binding 24 - **AC1**: Are actions/motions correctly attributed to the intended subject? 25 - **AC2**: Are directed spatial interactions correctly bound between intended subjects/objects (e.g., "A gives to B")? 26 27 ## Labeling Rules 28 29 - **[YES]**: binding relation is correct and stable. 30 - **[PARTIAL]**: mostly correct but temporarily ambiguous, partially inconsistent, or required binding relation is omitted entirely. 31 - **[NO]**: clearly incorrect for most or all of the video. 32 - **[N/A]**: not defined by the prompt/reference. 33 34 ## Scoring 35 36 [YES] = 5 [PARTIAL] = 3 [NO] = 1 [N/A] = omitted E.3 Audio-Visual Consistency Speech-Lip Synchronization (SLS) For speech-lip synchronization, we adopt two-stage evaluation pipeline. We first apply an MLLM-based pre-filter to retain only videos in which audible speech is produced by visible speaker with stable frontal or near-frontal face visibility and sufficient mouth visibility. This filtering step reduces unreliable SyncNet measurements caused by profile faces, occlusions, or off-screen speech. For the retained videos, we apply SyncNet-based VSLS pipeline on speech windows detected by voice activity detection (VAD). In multi-person scenes, candidate face tracks are first filtered according to their temporal offset. We then select the track with the lowest LSE-D as the speaking face, using lower absolute offset and higher LSE-C as tie-breakers. The selected track is further verified by crop-level stable-frontal check to ensure that the synchronization score is computed on reliable facial evidence. For each valid speech window, we normalize LSE-C as confidence score and reverse-normalize LSE-D as distance score. The final speech-lip synchronization score is computed as VSLS1-5 = 1 + 4 (0.60 Cnorm + 0.40 Dnorm) , where Cnorm denotes the normalized LSE-C score and Dnorm denotes the reverse-normalized LSE-D score. The resulting score is mapped to 15 scale, where higher values indicate better temporal synchronization between speech and visible lip motion.

sections.title: Stable Speaking Frontal Face Filtering Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Stable Speaking Frontal Face Judge 2 3 Judge whether the generated video is suitable for automatic speech-lip / VSLS evaluation. 4 5 Use only the generated video and its audio as evidence. Focus on the time span when speech or voice is audible. The item should pass only when the visible speaking person is consistently shown in half-body / upper-body / bust-style framing or closer, with stable frontal or near-frontal face while speaking. 6 7 ## Pass Condition 8 9 Set stable_speaking_frontal_face: true only if all conditions are met: 10 11 - audible speech or voice is present; 12 - the speaker is visible during the speech; 13 - the speaker is framed as half-body / upper-body / bust-style or closer during speech, not as tiny full-body or distant figure; 14 - the speakers face is frontal or near-frontal for most of the speaking duration; 15 - the mouth/lower face area is visible enough to support lip-sync evaluation; 16 - the face is not too small, heavily blurred, heavily occluded, cropped out, turned away, or shown mainly in profile; 17 - the shot is stable enough that the face can be tracked without frequent cuts, large camera motion, fast head turns, or severe scale changes; 18 - if multiple people are visible, the speaking person can be identified reliably. 19 20 ## Fail / Not Applicable 21 22 Set stable_speaking_frontal_face: false when: 23 24 - there is no audible speech; 25 - the speech is voiceover-like, off-screen, narrated, or not bound to visible speaker; 26 - the speaker is mainly side-facing, back-facing, too small, distant full-body, occluded, blurred, cropped, or outside the frame; 27 - the video cuts away from the face during speech; 28 - the mouth is not visible enough for lip-sync evaluation; 29 - multiple visible people could be speaking and the speaker cannot be identified reliably. 30 31 ## Scoring 32 33 - 5: clear pass; half-body/upper-body or closer framing with stable frontal or near-frontal speaking face for most speech. 34 - 4: mostly pass; minor pose/motion/framing issue but still suitable. 35 - 3: borderline; sometimes usable but not stable enough for strict automatic evaluation. 36 - 2: mostly unsuitable; face is often not frontal/stable/visible. 37 - 1: clearly unsuitable. Timbre Similarity (TS) Timbre Similarity evaluates whether the generated speech preserves the speaker timbre specified by the voice reference. For each applicable sample, we first isolate the generated speech segment and the reference-voice segment, and then extract speaker embeddings from both clips using the same speaker-verification backbone. The raw sample-level similarity is computed as the cosine similarity between the generated and reference speaker embeddings, after filtering out clips with insufficient speech duration (>2 s), severe overlap with non-speech audio, or failed voice-activity detection. To calibrate the scoring range, we synthesize auxiliary speech pairs with the same speaker timbre but different spoken content. We observe that their embedding cosine similarities typically fall in the range of 0.30 to 0.45, indicating that content variation can substantially lower the absolute similarity even when the perceived timbre remains consistent. Therefore, we treat cosine similarity above 0.30 as sufficiently strong evidence of timbre preservation and assign it the maximum score. For similarities below this threshold, we linearly map the cosine similarity to 15 scale, where higher scores indicate stronger timbre consistency. Event-Sound Matching (ESM) Visual Event-Sound Alignment Judge Prompt 1 # Visual Event-Sound Alignment MLLM Judge 2 3 You are senior Foley artist with 20 years of experience and an acoustic physicist. You are highly sensitive to sound textures produced by different materials (metal, wood, glass, liquids, fabric, etc.) under physical interactions, and you understand spatial acoustics (reverb characteristics). Your task is to assess the non-speech sound effects that are actually audible in the generated video. 4 5 Use only the generated video and its audio as evidence. Do not penalize an effect for being absent; evaluate only effects that are actually heard. 6 7 Evaluate two properties: 8 9 1. **Sound consistency:** Does the heard effect plausibly match the visible action, object, material, force, and environment? 10 2. **Temporal alignment:** Does the effect start, continue, repeat, and stop at the correct visible time? 11 12 Include: impacts, contacts, footsteps, clicks, scraping, pouring, rustling, object handling, doors, tools, and other Foley; scene-grounded environmental effects such as visible rain, wind-driven movement, traffic, birds, water, or machinery when they can be related to the visible scene. 13 14 Exclude: speech, dialogue, narration, voice identity, transcript accuracy, and lip synchronization; background music; inaudible effects or effects merely expected from an action; general visual quality. 15 16 ## Evaluation Rules 17 18 - First identify each distinct audible non-speech effect and pair it with the most plausible visible event or scene source. 19 - If an audible effect has no plausible visible counterpart, include it and mark it unrelated. 20 - Judge material only when visually inferable. Do not invent hidden materials or sources. 21 - Penalize only perceptible timing errors, not uncertain millisecond offsets. 22 - Do not penalize scene-grounded environmental sounds merely because their source is off-screen, as long as the sound is plausible for the visible environment. 23 - Do not lower the score only because matched sound is slightly quiet or partially masked, as long as it remains audible and its timing/source relation is clear. 24 25 ## Score Scale 26 27 - 1: clearly contradictory sound or severe timing failure 28 - 2: major mismatch or clearly early/late/disconnected timing 29 - 3: partially plausible but generic, ambiguous, or inconsistently aligned 30 - 4: appropriate and mostly aligned, with minor issue 31 - 5: clearly appropriate and synchronized Source Correctness (SC)

sections.title: Source Correctness Judge Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Source Correctness Judge 2 3 Judge only whether audible speech/voice can be reliably attributed to the expected visible speaker. 4 5 Do not judge transcript wording, language, lip-sync quality, mouth timing, speech timing, or reference identity similarity. Mouth/lip/jaw movement may be used only as positive evidence for identifying who is speaking. 6 7 ## Applicability 8 9 Score an item only when the speech source can be reliably bound to visible speaker. 10 11 Set source_match: "not_applicable" and score: null when: 12 13 - speech is off-screen, voiceover-like, narrated, or not visibly bound to speaker; 14 - no speech/voice is audible; 15 - the relevant speaker/source is too small, occluded, blurred, cropped, shown from behind, hidden by cutaway, or otherwise unreliable; 16 - multiple visible people could plausibly be speaking and no specific speaking source can be confirmed; 17 - the only evidence against the expected speaker is missing/unclear mouth movement, static mouth, poor lip sync, or timing mismatch. 18 19 Exception: if clear visible mouth/lip/jaw movement reliably identifies who is producing the audible speech, score the item normally even if the view is profile, partially occluded, or otherwise limited. 20 21 ## Scoring 22 23 - 1: clear positive evidence that different visible speaker is producing the speech. 24 - 3: expected speaker is reliably the source, but the voice gender clearly mismatches the expected speakers gender presentation. 25 - 5: expected speaker is reliably the source, with no clear voice-gender mismatch. 26 27 Do not penalize age, identity, role, accent, language, nationality, race, ethnicity, regional background, style, missing words, changed words, translated words, or incomplete speech. 28 29 Do not infer wrong_person merely because the expected speakers mouth does not move, lip sync is poor, or the speech sounds like voiceover. Use wrong_person only when another visible speaker is clearly producing the speech. Timbre Similarity (TS). For voice timbre similarity, we use an embedding-based automatic evaluation protocol. For each sample with voice reference, we extract the generated audio, convert both reference and generated speech to 16 kHz mono waveforms, and apply energy-based VAD to retain valid speech regions. Samples with less than 2 seconds of speech are excluded. We then use the SpeechBrain ECAPA-TDNN speaker embedding model to extract speaker embeddings and compute cosine similarity between the reference and generated speech. Since generated videos often contain short utterances, background audio, sound effects, and content different from the reference, the raw cosine values are lower than those in clean speaker verification. We therefore calibrate the score mapping using synthesized speech clips with the same timbre but different content, where cosine similarities typically fall between 0.30 and 0.45. Based on this observation, we treat cosine similarity above 0.30 as strong timbre consistency and map cosine scores to 15 scale with thresholds of 0.075, 0.150, 0.225, and 0.300. The final model-level score is averaged over all valid samples. E.

sections.title: Instruction Following
sections.content: Checklist generation prompt. Audio, Speech, and Temporal Checklist Generation Prompt 1 # IF Checklist Generation Prompt 2 3 You are an instruction-following checklist designer for reference-based video generation evaluation. 4 5 Convert the given video generation prompt into fixed evaluation checklist for **Audio Task Following**, **Speech Content Accuracy**, and **Temporal Order Following**. The checklist will be reused to evaluate multiple generated videos from different models, so it must be independent of any generated video. 6 7 ## Global Rules 8 9 - Generate checklist items only from the prompt and metadata. 10 - Do not infer anything from generated videos; none are provided. 11 - Do not invent requirements not stated or strongly implied by the prompt. 12 - Use the same checklist for all models for this sample. 13 14 ## Audio Task Following 15 16 Evaluates only explicitly requested non-speech audio. Include: music, ambience, environmental sounds, foley, object sounds, footsteps, rustling, clicks, movement sounds, silence when explicitly requested. Exclude: spoken dialogue, voice presence, speech content, speaker correctness, mouth movement, lip sync. 17 18 Each item must be **atomic** - tests exactly one independently verifiable audio requirement. Use 1-3 atomic questions when applicable. If the prompt only requires speech, mark Audio Task Following as not applicable. 19 20 ## Speech Content Accuracy 21 22 Evaluates exact spoken text requested by the prompt. Only include if exact speech text is required. Extract: expected speaker (e.g., @Image1), exact text in quotation marks, language (zh/en/unknown). Do not create items for vague requirements like "speaks" or "talks" when no exact text is provided. If no exact text required, mark as not applicable. 23 24 ## Temporal Order Following 25 26 Evaluates explicit temporal structure only. Generate questions for: 27 28 **A. Shot/Scene Order** - if the prompt explicitly describes multiple shots or scenes: one question for segmentation, one per required shot/scene, one for correct order. 29 30 **B. Explicit Action Order** - if two or more visible events have clear ordering words (first, then, before, after, finally, etc.): one atomic question per ordering constraint naming both events. 31 32 Do not infer order from sentence position. Do not include speech timing. Use 1-3 atomic questions when applicable. If neither applies, mark as not applicable. Visual Task Following (VT)

sections.title: Visual Checklist Generation Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Visual Checklist Generation Prompt 2 3 You are **Visual Instruction-Following Checklist Designer** for reference-based video generation evaluation. 4 5 Your task is to convert the given video generation prompt into fixed visual evaluation checklist. The checklist will be reused to evaluate multiple generated videos from different models, so it must be independent of any generated video. 6 7 ## Visual Task Following 8 9 Evaluate visible requirements explicitly stated in the prompt: subjects, objects, scene/environment, style, key physical actions, key expressions, and visual states. Only evaluate whether visual instructions are followed - do not evaluate audio, speech, lip sync, mouth motion, or background music. 10 11 For image/reference-based instructions, judge only whether the referenced subject/object/scene is present and used in the requested role. Do not evaluate visual resemblance, identity similarity, or texture fidelity (those belong to separate metrics). 12 13 ## Atomic Question Rule 14 15 Every checklist question must ask about **exactly one independently judgeable visual requirement**. Do not combine multiple requirements with and, or, while, or comma-lists. Split all multi-requirement sentences into separate questions. Never generate question for mouth movement, lip movement, speaking motion, or talking motion. 16 17 ## Checklist Generation Rules 18 19 - Generate items only from the input prompt; do not invent requirements. 20 - Generate 3-10 atomic questions when applicable. 21 - Generate questions for all explicit visual actions (except mouth/lip/speaking motion). 22 - For **action items**: use actor-neutral wording ("Does someone...", "Is the object...") - if different person performs the action, the item should still be satisfiable. 23 - For **appearance items** (clothing, outfit, accessories, hair, makeup): keep person-specific binding. 24 - Do not create items for tiny hand/finger motions, subtle expressions, exact gaze, precise pose angles, or minor background details unless they are core requirements. 25 - For hold/carry/use interactions, prioritize whether the subject visibly performs the functional role over exact object subtype or material. 26 - For multi-scene prompts, check only whether the content appears - not in which specific shot (shot order belongs to Temporal Order Following). 27 - If the prompt requires person to speak/say/talk, do not convert that into mouth-motion visual question; ignore it for Visual Task Following.

sections.title: Visual Task Following Judge Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Visual Task Following MLLM Judge 2 3 You are evaluating generated reference-based video for **Visual Task Following**. 4 5 Use only the generated video and the provided IF spec. This dimension evaluates only whether the requested **visible subjects, objects, scenes, actions, interactions, spatial relations, states, or framings** are present and used as instructed. 6 7 Do **not** evaluate: reference identity similarity, face similarity, clothing fidelity, texture fidelity, speech content, non-speech audio, or event order. 8 9 ## Evidence-First Scoring 10 11 For each checklist item, first identify the visible evidence - then assign the score. Do not decide score first and justify afterward. 12 13 Evidence analysis steps: 14 1. What requested subject/object/scene/action/relation/state/framing is visible? 15 2. What part of the requirement is satisfied? 16 3. What part is missing, unclear, incomplete, or contradicted? 17 4. Which evidence level best matches: no evidence / weak related evidence / partial / mostly complete / complete direct? 18 19 ## Scoring (1-5 per item) 20 21 - **1 = No evidence**: requirement completely absent, clearly wrong, or contradicted. 22 - **2 = Weak related evidence**: something relevant visible, but core requirement mostly missing. 23 - **3 = Partial evidence**: meaningful part visible, but an important part missing, incomplete, or incorrect. 24 - **4 = Mostly complete evidence**: core requirement clearly met, but some meaningful secondary detail missing or slightly wrong. 25 - **5 = Complete direct evidence**: full atomic requirement clearly, directly, and unambiguously shown. 26 27 Use **1** only when no related visible evidence exists. Use **5** only when the full requirement is directly and clearly visible. Most mixed, partial, or briefly shown cases **2, 3, or 4**. 28 29 ## Leniency Rules 30 31 Be lenient with small hand/finger motions, subtle expressions, exact gaze, precise pose angle, minor color/material differences, and incidental background details. For hold/carry/use actions, prioritize visible functional role over exact object subtype. Partial motion, visible action trend, plausible intermediate state, brief contact, or final state implying the action can support **4**. 32 33 Dimension-level score = arithmetic mean of applicable item scores. Audio Task Following (AT)

sections.title: Audio Task Following Judge Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Audio Task Following MLLM Judge 2 3 You are evaluating generated reference-based videos Audio Task Following. 4 5 Use only the generated video/audio and the provided IF spec. Do not reward content merely because it appears in the prompt. Do not penalize audio quality unless it prevents detecting whether requested sound is present. 6 7 Audio Task Following evaluates **only explicitly requested non-speech audio**: sound effects, ambience, environmental sounds, foley, object sounds, and background music. Speech Content Accuracy is handled separately - ignore speech, dialogue, narration, voiceover, spoken text, speaker identity, and lip sync. 8 9 ## Scoring Rules 10 11 - Use broad semantic matching. sound does not need an exact sound-library match; judge whether it plausibly matches the requested scene, action, or music requirement. 12 - Score 5: requested non-speech sound is clearly audible and broadly matches the requirement. 13 - Score 1: requested sound is clearly absent or clearly does not match. 14 - Scores 2-4: evidence is genuinely partial or uncertain, or only part of the requirement is present. 15 - Do not reduce the score merely because matching sound is faint, brief, partially masked, or not prominent. If still identifiable, give score 5. 16 - Do not penalize extra or unrequested audio. Unrelated speech, music, ambience, or sound effects must not lower the score. 17 18 ## Item Score Scale 19 20 - 1: Requested sound clearly absent or clearly incorrect. 21 - 2: Weak evidence; most of the requirement not satisfied. 22 - 3: Requested sound partially present or genuinely ambiguous. 23 - 4: Mostly present and recognizable, with minor substantive mismatch. 24 - 5: Clearly audible and broadly satisfies the requirement. 25 26 Dimension-level score = arithmetic mean of all applicable item scores. Speech Content Accuracy (SCA)

sections.title: Speech Content Accuracy Judge Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Speech Content Accuracy Judge 2 3 You are an expert in evaluating whether the speech content in generated videos accurately matches the requirements specified in the prompt. 4 5 Given video generation prompt and the generated video, evaluate how accurately the spoken content (dialogue, narration, voiceover, etc.) matches what was requested. 6 7 Focus **only** on accuracy of speech/spoken content. Do **not** evaluate: video visual quality, background music or sound effects, lip-sync alignment, tone or emotion (unless explicitly required), or whether the correct character/person speaks the line. 8 9 ## Handling @Image References 10 11 Treat every @ImageX reference as non-evaluable placeholder. Do not require the video to say the literal tag or replace it with the actual object/person name. Only evaluate the meaningful words **before, after, or between** placeholders - preserving the intended action, relation, negation, and order. 12 13 ## Evaluation Criteria 14 15 1. **Content Completeness** - Are all required words, sentences, or key information present? 16 2. **Content Correctness** - Is the spoken content free from substitutions, additions, or distortions? 17 3. **Language/Locale Match** - If language is specified, does the speech use that language? 18 4. **Ignore Speaker Assignment** - Do not penalize wrong speaker, swapped speakers, narrator voice, or off-screen voice. 19 20 ## Cross-language Translation Rule 21 22 If Chinese speech is required but the generated speech is semantically correct English translation, assign score **2**. Do not assign 2 when the translation changes, omits, or invents key information. 23 24 ## Scoring (1-5) 25 26 Score Description 27 -------------------- 28 5 All required speech content present and accurate in the correct language. 29 4 Minor omissions or slight paraphrasing that does not change the meaning. 30 3 Some content missing or partially inaccurate; key information mostly preserved but noticeably incomplete. 31 2 Significant portions missing or wrong; also for semantically correct but wrong-language translation. 32 1 Speech entirely absent, in the wrong language, or bears no relation to required content. Temporal Order Following (TO)

sections.title: Temporal Order Following Judge Prompt
sections.content: 1 # Temporal Order Following MLLM Judge 2 3 You are evaluating generated reference-based video for **Temporal Order Following**. 4 5 Use only the generated video and the provided IF spec. This dimension evaluates only whether the generated video follows the requested **temporal order, shot progression, scene progression, and required multi-shot structure**. 6 7 Do **not** evaluate: speech or dialogue timing; speech content; audio presence or correctness; visual resemblance or identity similarity; general visual task completion; reference consistency. 8 9 ## Item Type Classification 10 11 ### Type A: Order-comparison item 12 Asks whether one event/action/shot occurs before or after another. Evaluate only if both events are visible and assessable. If either is missing or unclear, mark applicable: false, score: null - missing event is not reversal. 13 14 ### Type B: Required-shot or required-scene presence item 15 Asks whether required shot or scene exists as recognizable distinct structural segment. If clearly absent, keep applicable: true and assign score **1**. 16 17 ### Type C: Shot/scene segmentation item 18 Asks whether the video has recognizable multi-shot or multi-scene structure. 19 20 ## Score Scale - Type (Order Comparison) 21 22 - 1: Both events visible, clearly reversed order. 23 - 2: Both events visible, substantial violation of requested order. 24 - 3: Both assessable, but overlap/repetition/weak boundaries prevent confident judgment. 25 - 4: Correct dominant order, but with brief displacement or minor overlap. 26 - 5: Requested order unambiguous and fully preserved. 27 28 ## Score Scale - Type & (Shot/Scene) 29 30 - 1: Required shot/scene absent or structurally contradicted. 31 - 2: Only weak or fragmentary attempt at the required shot/scene. 32 - 3: Partially recognizable but framing, boundaries, or placement is ambiguous. 33 - 4: Clearly present and distinct, with one meaningful minor issue. 34 - 5: Clearly present as recognizable distinct structural segment. 35 36 Dimension-level score = arithmetic mean of applicable and assessable item scores.
